BrACHeS

/ ˈbreɪks /  ·  Brake Assisted Cabin Heating System

Capturing and redirecting waste friction-heat from ventilated disc brakes to supplement cabin heating in electric vehicles, a passive zero-energy thermal recovery systemstem designed, modelled, and CFD-validated at IIT Kharagpur.

Author
Aman Tater
Project Type
Group Project
Department
Mechanical Engineering, IIT Kharagpur
Vehicle Reference
Maruti Suzuki Swift Dzire VDI
CAD & Simulation
SolidWorks · Ansys Fluent
Control System
Arduino Uno · LM-35 sensors
81%
Braking heat still wasted after RBS[1,2]
600℃
Peak disc temperature under hard braking[3]
3
Prototype iterations designed & simulated
42
Vanes per disc, Swift Dzire VDI

Abstract

Electric vehicles (EVs) continue to dissipate approximately 81% of braking energy as heat, even with Regenerative Braking Systems (RBS) recovering a portion.[1,2] BrACHeS (Brake Assisted Cabin Heating System) proposes to intercept the hot air expelled radially from ventilated disc brakes and route it into the vehicle cabin through an involute-shaped enclosure and an Arduino-controlled smart valve. Three prototype iterations were developed using SolidWorks and validated via Ansys Fluent CFD. The final enclosure is designed to the Swift Dzire VDI disc geometry, 3D-printed in four magnetically assembled sections, and governed by dual LM-35 temperature sensors. The system reduces the battery load of electrical cabin heating and extends heater element life, without requiring any additional energy input.

CFD Validated 3D Printed Prototype Arduino Control Zero Additional Energy Passive Recovery
Section 01

Introduction

The energy-loss problem in EV braking, the natural physics of ventilated discs, and the BrACHeS concept.

This project was developed as part of the General Championship Hardware Modelling Competition 2024 at IIT Kharagpur, representing Meghnad Saha Hall of Residence. The competition challenges teams to conceive, analyse, and prototype a novel hardware system; BrACHeS was the team’s entry, combining thermal engineering, fluid mechanics, and embedded control.

1.1Regenerative Braking and Its Limits

The widespread adoption of electric vehicles represents a pivotal shift toward sustainable transportation.[10] Despite significant advances, EVs still experience substantial energy loss during braking; kinetic energy is dissipated as heat through friction between brake pads and disc plates.[1] The Regenerative Braking System (RBS) mitigates this by running the traction motor as a generator to return energy to the battery.

However, at higher speeds or during aggressive braking, friction brakes must supplement RBS for adequate stopping power.[2] As a result, 81% of braking heat is still lost to the environment. RBS also becomes ineffective at low speeds and in downhill driving, and is curtailed when the battery is fully charged.[2]

Regenerative braking operates across three modes: during normal driving the traction motor drives the wheels; under light braking the motor reverses role, acting as a generator that converts kinetic energy back to electricity and simultaneously slows the vehicle; under hard braking, when the motor alone cannot provide sufficient stopping force, friction (hydraulic) brakes engage cooperatively alongside regenerative braking to meet the deceleration demand.[5]

1.2The BrACHeS Concept

This project introduces the Brake Assisted Cabin Heating System (BrACHeS), a novel approach that captures hot air expelled radially from ventilated disc plates during braking and redirects it to the vehicle cabin via a smart valve.[11] Regulation is managed by an Arduino Uno microcontroller reading two LM-35 temperature sensors. BrACHeS is a supplement to the vehicle’s existing heater, contributing thermal energy without consuming any additional electrical power.

1.3Disc Brake Physics: The Natural Airflow Source

Modern car disc brakes are ventilated: two annular plates separated by an air gap containing radial vanes.[4] The vanes serve a dual function: heat dissipation fins and centrifugal fan blades, drawing cool air axially inward at the hub and expelling heated air radially outward at the rim. Under heavy braking the disc can reach temperatures up to 600 °C.[3] Since the disc already generates a continuous hot radial airstream, BrACHeS captures and channels it rather than allowing it to disperse to atmosphere.

Section 02

Problem Formulation

Quantifying the unutilised thermal energy in EV braking and establishing the scope of BrACHeS.

2.1Braking Energy Budget

In conventional braking, every braking event is accompanied by considerable energy loss. Even with RBS deployed, only approximately 19% of total braking energy is recovered.[1,2] The remaining 81% is released as heat at the friction interface between brake pads and disc plates.

Recovered by RBS
~19%
Lost as friction heat
~81%
BrACHeS accessible share
partial

The friction heat is not uniformly dispersed; a significant fraction heats the air circulating through the ventilated disc. This heated airstream is directly accessible from outside the disc, making it the most practical target for thermal recovery without any modification to the braking system itself.

2.2Motivation: Cabin Heating in EVs
Battery Range Impact

Unlike ICE vehicles that use engine waste heat for free, EVs convert battery energy to heat. In cold climates this substantially reduces driving range.[12] Any supplementary heat source directly extends effective range.

Heater Element Life

Resistive heating elements undergo thermal fatigue under repeated full-load cycling. Supplementary brake heat reduces average operating load, lowering thermal stress and extending the heater’s service life.

2.3Why Not Thermoelectric Generators?

The first approach considered was converting brake heat to electricity using thermoelectric generators (TEGs), devices that exploit the thermoelectric (Seebeck) effect, typically built from Bismuth telluride (Bi₂Te₃) or Lead telluride (PbTe).[6]

i.
Low conversion efficiency
TEGs achieve only 5–8% conversion efficiency.[6] At such rates the recovered electrical energy would be negligible.
ii.
Temperature mismatch
TEGs require a hot-side temperature of 200–600 °C, only achievable under very heavy braking, not routine driving conditions.
iii.
Installation infeasibility
Any static heat-harvesting device near a rotating disc requires substantial brake assembly redesign, making practical implementation untenable.

Conclusion: heat energy should be used as heat, the most thermodynamically direct pathway: by capturing and routing the naturally generated hot airstream from the disc.

Section 03

System Design

Three prototype iterations, from vane redesign to the validated involute enclosure for the Swift Dzire VDI.

3.1Prototype 1: Vane Redesign (Discarded)

The first approach sought to modify the internal vane geometry of the disc brake, inspired by centrifugal compressor design.[7] Redesigned vanes were intended to redirect the natural radial outflow into an axial outflow along the disc axis, providing a directed hot-air stream at the hub for easy ducting.

Fig. 1a. Original disc plate (SolidWorks).
Fig. 1b. Redesigned compressor-inspired vane geometry.

Rejected CFD simulations yielded unsatisfactory results; the modified vanes did not produce the expected axial outflow. The intended outlet at the disc centre also coincides with the axle and wheel-hub connections, making ducting geometrically infeasible.

3.2Prototype 2: Involute Enclosure (Generic Disc)

A closer study of ventilated disc brake dynamics revealed that existing discs are already designed to expel air radially.[4] Rather than modifying the disc, the strategy shifted to enclosing it. The enclosure shape was drawn from centrifugal blower design[7], specifically the involute (volute) casing, which guides radially thrown air into a single directed outlet. Research on volute tongue clearance confirmed its direct influence on outlet velocity and blower efficiency.[8]

Fig. 2. Centrifugal blower schematic. The involute volute and tongue clearance are the key geometric features adapted for BrACHeS.[7]

Tongue Clearance

Tongue clearance is defined as the ratio of the minimum distance between the enclosure wall and the disc (acting as the impeller) to the disc outer diameter. Studies show this parameter significantly affects outlet velocity and blower efficiency.[8] Multiple designs were tested and the best performing variant was selected for progression to Prototype 3.

Fig. 3a. P2 isometric view.
Fig. 3b. Top view, involute profile.
Fig. 3c. Section view with disc vanes visible.

Validated CFD of Prototype 2 confirmed the viability of the involute enclosure concept. Parametric tongue-clearance studies and outlet velocity measurements provided the performance benchmarks for the final design.

3.3Prototype 3: BrACHeS Final Design (Swift Dzire VDI)

The final prototype was designed to the exact dimensions of the Swift Dzire VDI disc brake assembly. The enclosure combines a circular arc with three distinct involute curves at different sections to accommodate the brake caliper, steering column, and suspension geometry while fitting within the wheel rim.

Fig. 4a. BrACHeS Prototype 3, isometric CAD model.
Fig. 4b. Top view showing involute curve and vane layout.

Four-Piece Magnetic Assembly

The structure was divided into four 3D-printed sections, both to fit the printer build volume and to allow in-situ installation around an already-installed disc. Sections rejoin via embedded magnets, maintaining the involute geometry to adequate accuracy. Extensions on each section accommodate the magnets and create clean mating surfaces.

Part 1
Part 2
Part 3
Part 4
Fig. 5. Four 3D-printed sections of the Prototype 3 enclosure, assembled via embedded magnets.

Dimensions

Disc ParameterValue
Inner radius R₁74 mm
Outer radius R₂128 mm
Plate thickness7 mm
Inter-disc gap (b)8 mm
Vane thickness (t)6 mm
Number of vanes (N)42
Total brake radius149 mm
Enclosure FeatureDetail
Profile typeCirc. arc + 3 involute curves
Tongue clearance6% of outer diameter
Assembly method4-piece magnetic
Inner liningKapton tape (polyimide)
Outlet cross-sectionSquare
FabricationFDM 3D printing
Section 04

Calculations

Convective heat transfer analysis: deriving the outlet air temperature as a function of disc temperature.

4.1Governing Equation

Convection is the primary mode of heat transfer between the disc surface and the air flowing through the inter-vane passages. The convective heat transfer rate is given by Newton’s law of cooling:

Convective heat rate (Newton's law of cooling)
q̇  =  h · A · ΔT
q̇Rate of heat transfer[W]
hConvective heat transfer coefficient[W m⁻² K⁻¹]
AHeating surface area[m²]
ΔTTemperature difference (disc surface − air)[K or °C]
4.2Convective Coefficient h: Flow Regime

The expression for h depends on the Reynolds number Re, which characterises whether flow is laminar or turbulent. Through calculation and Ansys verification, flow inside the disc vane passages is confirmed to be turbulent (Re > 4000), so the turbulent correlation is used throughout.

Laminar (Re < 2300)
h  =  0.7 · KₐD · Re0.55
Turbulent (Re > 4000), used here
h  =  0.04 · KₐD · Re0.8
Reynolds number
Re  =  ρ · v · Deqη
KₐThermal conductivity of air[W m⁻¹ K⁻¹]
DOuter diameter of disc plate[m]
ρDensity of air = 1.1684 kg m⁻³[kg m⁻³]
vLocal air velocity[m s⁻¹]
DeqHydraulic (equivalent) diameter of vane passage[m]
ηDynamic viscosity of air = 1.837 × 10⁻⁵ Ns m⁻²[Pa s]
4.3Hydraulic Diameter of the Vane Passage

The inter-vane passage cross-section is approximately rectangular. Its hydraulic diameter Deq is evaluated at the mean disc radius Rm = (R₁ + R₂) / 2.

Fig. 6. Inner-view of disc plate: radii R₁, R₂, Rm and inter-vane angle θ.
Hydraulic diameter, rectangular duct
Deq  =  4 AP  =  2 · l · bl + b
lEffective circumferential passage width at Rm[m]
bGap between disc plates (= 8 mm)[m]
Derivation of effective passage width l
θ  =  2πN  →  l′  =  θ · Rm  =  2πN · Rm

l  =  l′ − t  =  2πN · Rm − t
θAngle between consecutive vanes[rad]
NNumber of vanes = 42n/a
RmMean radius = (74 + 128)/2 = 101 mm[m]
tVane thickness = 6 mm[m]

Numerical result:  l ≈ 9.1 mm,  Deq ≈ 8.5 mm.

4.4Differential Heat Balance

Considering an infinitesimal strip of air of width dx moving radially through the vane passage, the heat balance equates the enthalpy gained by the air to the convective heat flux from the disc surface:

Differential heat balance (after simplification)
dTTd − Ta  =  2 h (l + b)ρ · A · v · Cp  ·  dy
TdLocal disc surface temperature[°C]
TaLocal air temperature[°C]
CpSpecific heat of air at constant pressure = 1004.61 J kg⁻¹ K⁻¹[J kg⁻¹ K⁻¹]
yRadial distance from disc centre[m]
4.5Empirical Velocity Correlation

Radial outlet air velocity was measured at multiple disc angular speeds. A linear fit through the origin was derived:

Radial escape velocity vs. angular speed (measured + fitted)
v  =  0.02899 · ω
vRadial air escape speed at disc rim[m s⁻¹]
ωAngular velocity of the disc[rad s⁻¹]
Fig. 7. Radial outlet air speed (V) versus disc angular speed (ω). Measured data points (circles) and linear regression fit (orange line): v = 0.02899ω.
4.6Final Temperature Equation & Results

Substituting the turbulent h correlation, empirical velocity relation, and Swift Dzire VDI disc dimensions, integration of the heat balance from R₁ to R₂ yields:

Outlet air temperature as a function of disc temperature
Tair  =  Tdisc − e−0.012326 · (Tdisc − 5 °C)
Fig. 8. Outlet air temperature (Tair, °C) versus disc temperature (Tdisc, °C). Key computed coordinates annotated.
Tdisc (°C)Tair (°C)
505.551
1006.164
1506.776
2007.389
2508.001
3008.614
3509.226
Air PropertyValue
Pressure10⁵ Pa
Ref. temperature298.15 K
Density ρ1.1684 kg m⁻³
Viscosity η1.837 × 10⁻⁵ Pa s
Spec. heat Cp1004.61 J kg⁻¹ K⁻¹
Conductivity Kₐ2.67 × 10⁻² W m⁻¹ K⁻¹

These values are conservative estimates based on a single disc at ambient reference conditions. Operation with all four discs and higher disc temperatures from aggressive braking would yield substantially greater thermal contribution to the cabin.

Section 05

CFD Simulation

Ansys Workbench / Fluent used to validate each prototype design before physical fabrication.

5.1Methodology
01
Geometry: SolidWorks
Each prototype modelled parametrically. Prototype 1: modified vane disc. Prototypes 2 & 3: disc enclosed in the involute casing. Prototype 3 additionally incorporated brake caliper, steering column, and suspension geometry.
02
Boundary Conditions: Ansys Fluent
Inlet: axial velocity at the hub opening (one side closed for wheel connection). Outlet: 1 atm static pressure at the enclosure duct exit. Disc walls: rotating reference frame at specified ω (rad s⁻¹).
03
Prototype 1: Failed
No meaningful axial outflow achieved. Geometric conflict with the axle confirmed the approach was infeasible. Design abandoned.
04
Prototype 2: Concept Validated
Involute enclosure produced organised spiral flow with high outlet velocity. Parametric tongue-clearance sweep identified the 6% optimum for Prototype 3.
05
Prototype 3: Final Validation
Car-specific geometry with all hardware obstructions included. Boundary conditions set to match demonstration operating speed. Satisfactory velocity and pressure profiles confirmed. Design finalised for fabrication.
5.2Velocity Streamlines: Ansys Fluent Results

The four velocity streamline visualisations below show the airflow pattern inside the involute enclosure at final operating conditions. Colour scale ranges from 0 (blue) to 26.12 m s⁻¹ (red). The high-velocity zone (yellow/red) concentrates near the outlet duct, confirming the enclosure’s effectiveness in directing and accelerating the flow.

View A
View B
View C
View D
Fig. 9. Velocity streamlines in the BrACHeS involute enclosure (Ansys Fluent). Scale: 0 (blue) to 26.12 m s⁻¹ (red). Concentrated high-velocity region at the outlet duct confirms directed thermal flow.
5.3Pressure Distribution

The pressure volume rendering illustrates the pressure field inside the enclosure. Elevated pressure (up to +8.07 kPa) at the disc outer rim confirms the centrifugal pumping action of the spinning disc. The negative pressure zone (−3.03 kPa) near the outlet drives sustained outward flow; no external pump is required.

Fig. 10. Pressure volume rendering (Ansys Fluent). Scale: −3.03 kPa (blue) to +8.07 kPa (teal). Centrifugal pumping at the rim sustains the radial-to-outlet flow.
Section 06

Control Logic & Valve System

The Arduino-driven smart valve and the three-condition decision gate governing when warm air enters the cabin.

6.1Air Channelling: Enclosure to Cabin

After exiting the involute enclosure, hot air passes through a smart valve with two outlets: one to the atmosphere and one to the vehicle cabin. The valve body is constructed from copper pipe covered with nitrile insulation foam (standard HVAC ducting practice[9]) to minimise heat loss in transit. The enclosure inner surface is lined with Kapton tape (polyimide film) to reduce conductive losses through the enclosure walls.

Fig. 11a. Valve closed: flow directed to atmosphere.
Fig. 11b. Valve open: flow redirected to cabin inlet.
6.2Valve Control Logic

The smart valve is controlled by an Arduino Uno reading two LM-35 temperature sensors, one inside the cabin and one at the enclosure outlet. A servo motor actuates the valve. The valve opens only when all three conditions are simultaneously satisfied; otherwise air is vented to atmosphere.

Fig. 12. Valve control logic flowchart. Three conditions must all be true for the valve to open: (1) heater ON, (2) Tcabin < Tset − 2.5 °C, (3) Toutlet > Tcabin.

Hysteresis note: the 2.5 °C dead-band around the cabin set-point prevents rapid valve cycling when the cabin temperature hovers near the target, improving both comfort and servo longevity.

6.3Circuit Diagram & Hardware
Electronic ComponentDetail
MicrocontrollerArduino Uno
Temperature sensors2× LM-35 (10 mV °C⁻¹)
Valve actuatorServo motor
Display16×2 LCD
Filter capacitor10 µF
Mechanical ComponentDetail
Valve body insulationCopper + nitrile foam[9]
Transmission pipe (demo)Domestic LPG hose
Enclosure inner liningKapton tape (polyimide)
Valve outlets2 (cabin / atmosphere)
Section 07

Conclusion

Outcomes, dual benefit, limitations, and future scope of BrACHeS.

7.1Summary of Outcomes
3
Prototypes designed, simulated, evaluated
CFD ✓
Ansys Fluent validation of final design
0 W
Additional energy input required
6%
Optimal tongue clearance identified

BrACHeS demonstrates a viable, low-complexity, energy-passive approach to recovering waste thermal energy from disc braking. The involute enclosure successfully captures and directs the naturally generated hot radial airstream; CFD confirms adequate outlet velocity and pressure gradient; and the Arduino control system ensures the cabin receives warm air only under appropriate conditions.

7.2Dual Benefit
Battery Range Preserved

Supplementing cabin heating with otherwise wasted brake heat reduces the electrical drain on the battery pack. In cold climates, where cabin heating can consume a substantial fraction of range[12]; even partial supplementation meaningfully extends driving distance.

Heater Element Life Extended

Operating the resistive heater at reduced average load lowers thermal cycling stress and peak current demand. BrACHeS acts as a thermal support system, reducing degradation of the heating element and extending its service life.

7.3Limitations
▶
Intermittent heat supply
Brake heat is only available during active friction braking. The system supplements, not replaces, the primary heater. Benefit is greatest in stop-and-go urban driving with frequent braking events.
▶
Moderate outlet temperature (single disc)
The analytical model yields outlet air 6–10 °C above ambient for typical conditions; sufficient for supplementary contribution but not standalone heating. Extension to all four discs would multiply the thermal flux proportionally.
7.4Future Scope
01
Scale to all four discs
Each disc is independent and uses the same enclosure geometry. Equipping all four (or even both front discs, which generate more heat) would multiply the heat flux and substantially increase supplementation effectiveness.
02
OEM integration via CAN bus
The Arduino control logic maps directly to a vehicle ECU. Integration with the OEM HVAC controller via CAN bus would allow seamless blending of brake-recovered heat and resistive heat with no driver intervention.[13]
03
Drive-cycle energy audit
A WLTP- or NEDC-based drive-cycle thermal simulation would quantify kWh diverted per journey, enabling a rigorous range-extension and CO₂ reduction estimate to support the commercial case.

BrACHeS demonstrates that, even after regenerative braking recovers its ≈19% share, accessible thermal energy remains in the disc airstream. Treating this stream as a resource rather than waste contributes to a more complete energy utilisation strategy for electric vehicles, consistent with the goals of sustainable and efficient transportation.[10] The iterative, simulation-validated methodology (from TEG rejection through three prototype generations) produced a physically realisable, low-complexity addition to EV braking infrastructure with measurable energy and comfort benefits.

Aman Tater · Undergraduate, Department of Mechanical Engineering · Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur · Collaborative group project
